zh:
朝鲜半岛的神话沿两条脉络传到今天。一条在纸上：高丽僧人一然的《三国遗事》（1281年）与李承休的《帝王韵纪》（1287年）在蒙古压境的年代里追记檀君开国，把半岛的历史起点安放在公元前2333年；更早的金富轼《三国史记》（1145年）与立于414年的广开土王碑，则记下朱蒙破卵而生、鱼鳖架桥的高句丽建国故事。天神下降、感日光而孕、英雄破卵而出，是这批建国神话反复出现的母题。

另一条脉络在巫觋的口中。半岛的巫俗至今仍在跳神仪式（굿）里通宵演唱长篇叙事巫歌：被抛弃的巴里公主远赴西天求药，身后引渡亡魂；未婚而孕的당금애기成为产神三神奶奶；济州岛更号称有一万八千神，村村传唱自家堂神的本解（본풀이）。这些神话没有经典定本，全凭一代代巫觋口耳相传，二十世纪初才开始被大规模记录。

在文献与巫歌之外是民谭的世界：旧扫帚化成的独脚鬼怪半夜拦人摔跤，修炼千年的九尾狐扮作新娘，白衣散发的处女鬼一遍遍回来找人听她鸣冤，老虎翻过一道道山梁讨年糕吃。这些故事关心的都是具体的人间事——冤屈要昭雪，弃儿会救亲，贪心人挨罚。半岛民谭里的老虎既凶恶又时常受愚弄，鬼多是屈死之人，神多是熬过大苦难的女子。
en:
The myths of the Korean peninsula have come down along two separate channels. One is written: the monk Iryeon's Samguk Yusa (1281) and Yi Seung-hyu's Jewang Ungi (1287), compiled under Mongol pressure, reach back to Dangun's founding of Gojoseon and anchor the peninsula's history at 2333 BCE, while Kim Bu-sik's Samguk Sagi (1145) and the Gwanggaeto Stele of 414 CE preserve the Goguryeo founding tale of Jumong, hatched from an egg and carried across a river on a bridge of fish and turtles. Gods descending from heaven, conception by pursuing sunlight, heroes breaking out of eggs: these are the recurring motifs of the foundation cycle.

The other channel runs through the mouths of shamans. Korean mu practice still sings long narrative epics through the night at gut rituals: the abandoned Princess Bari journeys to the western sky for the water of life and becomes the guide of dead souls; the unwed mother Danggeum-aegi becomes Samsin, grandmother of childbirth; Jeju Island claims eighteen thousand gods, each village chanting the bon-puri of its own shrine deity. These myths have no canonical text and were passed shaman to shaman, only being systematically transcribed from the early twentieth century.

Beyond chronicle and chant lies the world of folktales: dokkaebi goblins risen from old brooms demand midnight wrestling matches, thousand-year foxes dress as brides, white-robed maiden ghosts return night after night for someone willing to hear their grievance, and a tiger begs rice cakes at every mountain pass. The stories dwell on concrete human business, wrongs to be righted, abandoned children who save their parents, the greedy getting their due. The peninsula's tigers are fearsome and frequently fooled, its ghosts are mostly the wrongfully dead, and its gods are, again and again, women who survived great suffering.
